g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Completely Randomized Design, a fundamental method for comparing treatment groups in experiments.
CRD is an experimental design where treatments are randomly assigned to experimental units, ensuring unbiased comparisons between groups.
g.text: CRD is a fundamental experimental design method where all subjects are randomly assigned to
different treatment groups. It ensures that any differences between groups can be attributed
to the treatments themselves, not to pre-existing differences.

g.data-narration: Four essential concepts form the foundation of CRD: treatment groups, control group, randomization, and replication.
CRD has three primary components: treatment groups for conditions under investigation, control group as baseline, and randomization.
CRD relies on three key assumptions: independence of observations, homogeneity of variance, and normal distribution.
The analysis of CRD follows four key steps: data collection, hypothesis testing, statistical tests, and post-hoc analysis.
Consider a CRD example testing three training programs (A, B, C) on algorithm performance with different numbers of subjects.
g.text: Testing the efficacy of three different training programs on improving algorithm performance.

g.data-narration: We formulate two hypotheses: null hypothesis states no difference between groups, while alternative states at least one group differs.
g.text: μ₁ = μ₂ = μ₃ (No significant difference between groups)
At least one μᵢ ≠ μⱼ (At least one group mean differs)

g.data-narration: One-way ANOVA tests if at least one group mean is significantly different from others by comparing treatment variance to error variance.
g.text: One-way ANOVA tests whether there are statistically significant differences between the means
of at least two groups by comparing variance between treatments to variance within groups.

g.data-narration: The ANOVA table contains key formulas: SS Treatments measures between-group variation, MS is SS divided by degrees of freedom.
When ANOVA shows significant differences, Tukey's HSD test identifies which specific pairs of group means differ significantly.
g.text: If ANOVA rejects H₀, Tukey's HSD (Honest Significant Difference) test determines which specific
pairs of group means are significantly different from each other.
A vs B | A vs C | B vs C

g.data-narration: The complete CRD workflow flows from randomization through data collection, ANOVA analysis, to post-hoc testing for conclusions.
Key formulas to remember: SS Treatments measures between-group variation, MS divides by degrees of freedom, F is the ratio.
CRD provides a straightforward approach for comparing treatments. By ensuring randomization and balanced variances, CRD enables robust statistical analysis.
g.text: • CRD ensures unbiased treatment comparisons through random assignment
• Three assumptions must be met: independence, homogeneity of variance, and normality
• ANOVA tests for significant differences; Tukey's HSD identifies specific group differences
Master CRD for robust experimental analysis!
